JUb19 as donor versus all other recipients

Data formating from pairwise competition

Compile data from different competition assays (MSPm1 as donor and other species as recipient) at start (0 hr) and end (24 hr) points (under OD = 1).

Frequency lineplot (MSPm1 vs others)

Here, as a first attempt to visualize the competitive outcome, we plot the frequency change across 0 and 24 hrs. The first figure shows the frequency change for MSPm1 (donor) and the second mirrored figure shows that for the other species (recipient). As expected, MSPm1 is indeed much less superior compared to the previous competition experiments involving JUb19. The frequency of MSPm1 plummets when competing with JUb19 and MYb10.

Interspecies competition value between donor and recepient (MSPm1 vs others)

Again, consider a Lotkta–Volterra competition model in the form of \(\frac{dN_i}{dt} = N_i(r_i-\alpha_{ii}N_i-\alpha_{ij}N_j)\). While we used a statistical fitting approach to estimate the intraspecific competition coefficients (\(\alpha_{ii}\)H), we cannot do that for the interspecific competition coefficients (\(\alpha_{ij}\)) due to the lack of a density gradient (but see the results for Jub19 and CEN2ent in a separate file). Instead, we use a “hybrid” approach. Here, in the Lotka–Volterra competition model, the per capita growth rate is still estimated as \(\frac{\Delta lnN_i}{\Delta t}\), and the species’ \(r_i\) and \(\alpha_{ii}\) are obtained by the previous statistical fitting results for the monoculture growth assay (i.e., \(\hat{r_i}\) and \(\hat{\alpha_{ii}}\)). By rearranging the equation, we can indirectly obtain an esitmate for the interspecific competition coefficients as follows:
\[\alpha_{DR} = \left[\hat{r_D}-\hat{\alpha_{DD}}N_{D0}^c-\left(\frac{lnN_{D24}^c - lnN_{D0}^c}{24}\right)\right].\left(\frac{1}{N_{R0}^c}\right)\] \[\alpha_{RD} = \left[\hat{r_R}-\hat{\alpha_{RR}}N_{R0}^c-\left(\frac{lnN_{R24}^c - lnN_{R0}^c}{24}\right)\right].\left(\frac{1}{N_{D0}^c}\right)\], where \(N_{jt}^c\) represents the scaled population size of species \(j\) (= \(D\) of \(R\)) at time \(t\) (= 0 or 24 hr) within the co-culture (superscript \(c\)). Note again all density data were scaled by \(10^9\) prior to calculation. Below, we show (left) the estimated effect of other recipient species of MSPm1 (\(\alpha_{DR}\)), and (right) the estimated effect of MSPm1 on the other recipient species (\(\alpha_{RD}\), with \(D\) = MSPm1). Interestingly, the effect of MSPm1 on both JUb19 and MYb10 are weak compared to reciprocal interactions, which may explain why MSPm1 plummets when competed against these two species.
